name: San Marino
tagline: Liberty on a mountaintop: the world's oldest surviving republic, founded by a stonemason in 301.
summary: San Marino traces its existence to the year 301, when, by tradition, a Christian stonemason named Marinus fled the persecutions of the emperor Diocletian and founded a small community on the slopes of Mount Titano. From that legend grew a self-governing commune whose institutions, above all the two Captains Regent elected every six months, have endured with remarkable continuity for some seventeen centuries, giving San Marino its claim to be the world's oldest surviving sovereign republic. Protected by its mountain isolation and a tradition of shrewd, watchful diplomacy, the little state survived the rise and fall of the Papal States, the unification of Italy that closed in around it, and the upheavals of the modern age. Napoleon offered to enlarge it and was politely refused; Abraham Lincoln was made an honorary citizen; Giuseppe Garibaldi was given refuge; and in the Second World War the republic, though neutral and bombed, sheltered some hundred thousand refugees. Today San Marino is a UN member that uses the euro and mints its own coins, an enclave entirely surrounded by Italy whose freedom rests on treaties of friendship and on a continuity unbroken since the Middle Ages.

founding.label: The founding of the community on Mount Titano by Saint Marinus
founding.detail: By tradition the Republic of San Marino was founded on 3 September in the year 301, when Marinus, a Christian stonemason of Dalmatian origin who had fled the anti-Christian edicts of the emperor Diocletian, established a small free community on Mount Titano. Legend holds that on his deathbed he left his followers the words 'Relinquo vos liberos ab utroque homine' - 'I leave you free from both men' - understood as freedom from both emperor and pope, a phrase enshrined as the founding ideal of Sammarinese liberty. The traditional date is the basis of San Marino's claim to be the world's oldest surviving sovereign republic, a continuity that UNESCO recognised in 2008 when it inscribed the Historic Centre and Mount Titano as a 'testimony to the continuity of a free republic since the Middle Ages'. The first documentary trace of the community is far later, in the Placito Feretrano of 885, but the institutional life that grew on the mountain has run, in one form or another, ever since.

quickFacts.label: Traditional founding
quickFacts.value: Founded by Saint Marinus on Mount Titano, by tradition in AD 301

quickFacts.label: Heads of state
quickFacts.value: Two Captains Regent (Capitani Reggenti), elected every six months

quickFacts.label: Written constitution
quickFacts.value: Leges Statutae of 8 October 1600, among the oldest governing documents still in force

quickFacts.label: Independence from Italy
quickFacts.value: Guaranteed by treaties of friendship beginning in 1862, renewed in 1939 and after

quickFacts.label: United Nations
quickFacts.value: Admitted on 2 March 1992; uses the euro and mints its own coins by agreement

eras.title: Saint Marinus and the Mountain Commune
eras.standfirst: A stonemason fleeing persecution gave his name to a community on Mount Titano whose love of freedom outlasted the empire that drove him there.
body: According to a tradition the Sammarinese have cherished for centuries, the republic was born in the year 301, when a Christian stonemason called Marinus, of Dalmatian origin, fled the persecutions of the emperor Diocletian and climbed Mount Titano, a limestone massif rising above the plain near Rimini. There he and a small band of companions established a free community of believers. Marinus would later be venerated as a saint, and the place and people took his name; on his deathbed, the legend records, he bequeathed his followers their guiding principle, leaving them 'free from both men' - free, that is, from the authority of both emperor and pope.

Whatever the precise truth of the foundation story, the community on the mountain is genuinely ancient. The earliest surviving document to mention it, the Placito Feretrano of 885, records a dispute in which the monastery and the men of San Marino defended their liberties, showing that by the ninth century a self-conscious community already existed on Titano. Around it grew the legend of the Lady Felicissima, a wealthy woman whose family, in gratitude to Marinus, was said to have given the mountain to his community, an origin tale that expressed the Sammarinese conviction that their freedom had been theirs from the very beginning.

The mountain itself was the community's first protector. Steep, fortifiable and of little strategic value to the great powers of the Italian peninsula, Mount Titano allowed a tiny population to govern itself while empires, kingdoms and the lands of the Church rose and fell in the surrounding country. Out of this isolation, and out of the habit of collective self-government in the assembly of family heads known as the Arengo, grew the institutions that would in time make San Marino a republic in fact as well as in name.

events.title: Foundation by Saint Marinus
events.summary: By tradition the stonemason Marinus, fleeing Diocletian's persecution of Christians, founded a free community on Mount Titano on 3 September 301, the act from which San Marino dates its existence.

events.title: 'I leave you free from both men'
events.summary: Marinus is said to have left his followers the words 'Relinquo vos liberos ab utroque homine', freedom from both emperor and pope, enshrined as the founding ideal of Sammarinese liberty.

events.title: The Placito Feretrano
events.summary: The earliest surviving document to name the community of San Marino records a legal dispute over its liberties, the first firm attestation that a self-governing community existed on Mount Titano.

events.title: Refuge on a fortified mountain
events.summary: Set atop the steep limestone massif of Mount Titano and of little strategic value to its powerful neighbours, the community could govern itself in isolation, a continuity of free republican life that UNESCO would later recognise as stretching from the Middle Ages to today.

figures.name: Saint Marinus
figures.blurb: A Christian stonemason of Dalmatian origin who, by tradition, fled Diocletian's persecution and founded a free community on Mount Titano in 301. His deathbed injunction to remain 'free from both men' became the republic's founding ideal, and he is venerated as its patron saint.

figures.name: The Lady Felicissima
figures.blurb: In the medieval legend of San Marino's origins, a wealthy woman whose family, in gratitude to Marinus, gave Mount Titano to his community, a tale that expressed the Sammarinese conviction that their freedom had been theirs from the start.

pullquote.text: Relinquo vos liberos ab utroque homine - I leave you free from both men.
pullquote.attribution: Words traditionally ascribed to Saint Marinus

eras.title: The Commune, the Statutes and the Captains Regent
eras.standfirst: The mountain settlement became a self-governing commune, devised the dual headship of the Captains Regent, and in 1600 set its liberties down in a written constitution that still stands.
body: Over the medieval centuries the community on Titano organised itself as a commune in the Italian fashion, governing through the Arengo of household heads and, increasingly, through an elected council. Its most distinctive invention was the office of the Captains Regent, the Capitani Reggenti: by tradition two heads of state, chosen together and serving for only six months, so that no individual could entrench personal power. The arrangement, in place since the statute of the late thirteenth century, has endured to the present day, with the Regents entering office each year on 1 April and 1 October. Early statutes were drawn up in 1253 and a fuller body of law by 1295.

San Marino's survival owed as much to diplomacy as to its walls. Surrounded by the territories of powerful neighbours and, above all, by the expanding Papal States, the little republic preserved its independence by careful alliances, by playing rivals against one another, and by securing recognitions of its rights from popes and princes. It weathered the ambitions of the lords of Rimini and Urbino and the long shadow of papal Romagna, repeatedly affirming a freedom that larger powers found it convenient, or unprofitable, to leave alone.

The crowning act of the period was constitutional. On 8 October 1600 the republic promulgated the Leges Statutae Republicae Sancti Marini, the Statutes of San Marino, a codified body of law in six books regulating the powers of the Council and the Captains Regent and the conduct of civil and criminal justice. Among the oldest written governing documents still in force anywhere in the world, the Statutes remain to this day at the foundation of Sammarinese law, the legal embodiment of a republic that had governed itself for more than a thousand years.

events.title: First Captains Regent
events.summary: The republic adopted its signature institution, two Captains Regent serving jointly for six-month terms; since the late-thirteenth-century statute their office has alternated every six months without interruption.

events.title: The earliest Statutes
events.summary: San Marino set down its first written statutes, with a fuller body of state law following by 1295, formalising the self-government of the mountain commune.

events.title: Self-government affirmed within papal Romagna
events.summary: As the Papal States consolidated their hold over the surrounding Romagna, San Marino preserved its autonomy by diplomacy and the recognition of its rights, the strategy of survival that kept the commune free among far greater powers.

events.title: Promulgation of the Leges Statutae
events.summary: The republic enacted the Leges Statutae Republicae Sancti Marini, a six-book written constitution governing the Council, the Regents and the courts; it remains in force as the foundation of San Marino's law.

figures.name: The Captains Regent (Capitani Reggenti)
figures.blurb: San Marino's two co-equal heads of state, elected by the Grand and General Council and serving together for only six months. Devised to prevent any one person monopolising power, the office is among the oldest continuously functioning institutions of republican government in the world.

figures.name: The Arengo
figures.role: Assembly of the heads of families
figures.blurb: The ancient assembly of household heads that exercised direct self-government on Mount Titano before power passed to the Grand and General Council. Dormant for centuries, it would be revived in 1906 to dismantle oligarchy and remains a channel for citizens' petitions.

pullquote.text: On 8 October 1600 the first written constitution was promulgated, the Leges Statutae Republicae Sancti Marini, which continues to remain at the foundation of San Marino law.
pullquote.attribution: Consulate of San Marino, on the Statutes of 1600

eras.title: The Alberoni Occupation and Restored Liberty
eras.standfirst: When the Papal States closed in around it, San Marino lost its independence for five months in 1739, only to win it back by civil resistance and a pope's own judgement.
body: By the seventeenth century San Marino was an island of self-government within the lands of the Church, which had absorbed the surrounding duchies and lordships. The republic guarded its autonomy jealously and secured papal acknowledgements of its rights, but its position was always precarious, dependent on the goodwill of a far greater neighbour. That precariousness was tested dramatically in 1739.

On 17 October 1739 Cardinal Giulio Alberoni, the papal legate of Ravenna, marched troops into San Marino, suspended its constitution and tried to force the republic to submit to the government of the Papal States, abolishing the Captains Regent in favour of a single governor of his own appointment. The Sammarinese did not take up arms. Instead they resorted to civil disobedience, refused to recognise the new order, and smuggled out appeals to Rome documenting the cardinal's abuses, trusting the justice of the pope over the ambition of his legate.

The gamble succeeded. Informed of the unrest and of Alberoni's procedural violations, Pope Clement XII found in the republic's favour: he condemned the occupation, ordered the withdrawal of the troops, and on 5 February 1740 - the feast of Saint Agatha, henceforth a national holiday - restored San Marino's independence. The episode became a founding story of the modern republic, proof that a tiny state, armed only with its law and its stubbornness, could recover its freedom even from the Papacy.

events.title: An island within the Papal States
events.summary: By the seventeenth century the lands of the Church surrounded San Marino on every side; the republic guarded its autonomy and secured papal acknowledgements of its rights, but its independence rested on the goodwill of a far greater neighbour.

events.title: The Alberoni occupation
events.summary: Cardinal Giulio Alberoni, papal legate of Ravenna, occupied San Marino with troops, suspended its constitution and sought to annex it to the Papal States, abolishing the Captains Regent.

events.title: Civil resistance
events.summary: Rather than fight, the Sammarinese practised civil disobedience and sent clandestine appeals to Rome documenting the legate's abuses, refusing to accept the imposed government.

events.title: Independence restored by Clement XII
events.summary: Pope Clement XII condemned the occupation and on 5 February 1740 recognised San Marino's rights and restored its independence, an event still celebrated each year on the feast of Saint Agatha.

figures.name: Pope Clement XII
figures.role: Pope who restored San Marino's independence
figures.blurb: Reigning pope at the time of the Alberoni occupation. Persuaded by the Sammarinese appeals and informed of his legate's violations, he condemned the seizure and on 5 February 1740 restored San Marino's independence.

figures.name: Cardinal Giulio Alberoni
figures.role: Papal legate who occupied San Marino
figures.blurb: Papal legate of Ravenna who in October 1739 seized San Marino with troops, abolished the Captains Regent and tried to annex the republic to the Papal States. His occupation collapsed within months when Pope Clement XII condemned it and ordered the troops withdrawn.

pullquote.text: The Pope recognised the rights of San Marino and on 5 February 1740 he restored the country's independence.
pullquote.attribution: On the end of the Alberoni occupation

eras.title: Napoleon, Garibaldi and Lincoln: a Republic Among Friends
eras.standfirst: As revolution and unification redrew the map of Italy, San Marino kept its independence by refusing aggrandisement, sheltering a hero of the Risorgimento, and winning the friendship of the United States.
body: The Napoleonic upheaval that swept away the old order in Italy left San Marino not diminished but admired. In 1797 Napoleon Bonaparte, far from threatening the little republic, offered it his friendship, gifts of artillery, and an extension of its territory toward the sea. With what tradition remembers as instinctive wisdom, the Sammarinese accepted the courtesies but declined the land, content, they said, with the borders they already had, unwilling to provoke envy or entanglement. San Marino's independence was recognised by Napoleon in the Treaty of Tolentino of 1797 and again by the Congress of Vienna in 1815.

The republic's reputation as a refuge was sealed in the Risorgimento. In the summer of 1849, after the fall of the Roman Republic, the revolutionary general Giuseppe Garibaldi, hunted by Austrian, French, Spanish and Neapolitan forces, crossed into San Marino with his remaining followers and his wife Anita. The Captain Regent Domenico Maria Belzoppi granted them asylum on condition that they lay down their cause within the republic's borders; Garibaldi rested, then slipped away by night toward the Adriatic. San Marino had risked occupation to honour its tradition of sanctuary.

From across the Atlantic came an enduring token of esteem. In 1861 the Captains Regent offered honorary citizenship to the new American president, Abraham Lincoln. In a letter of 7 May 1861, written as the United States slid into civil war, Lincoln accepted, replying that 'although your dominion is small, your State is nevertheless one of the most honored, in all history', and that San Marino proved a government founded on republican principles could be 'secure and enduring'. The exchange marked the beginning of United States recognition of San Marino. After the unification of Italy, a treaty of friendship in 1862 confirmed the republic's independence, the first of a series of such agreements with its now all-encircling neighbour.

events.title: Napoleon's offer declined
events.summary: Napoleon offered San Marino friendship, artillery and an extension of territory to the sea; the republic accepted the gifts but declined the land, satisfied with its existing borders, and was recognised by the Treaty of Tolentino.

events.title: Recognition at the Congress of Vienna
events.summary: The post-Napoleonic Congress of Vienna confirmed San Marino's independence, leaving the ancient republic untouched as Europe's monarchies were restored around it.

events.title: Garibaldi given refuge
events.summary: Fleeing the collapse of the Roman Republic and pursued by four armies, Giuseppe Garibaldi crossed into San Marino with his followers and was granted asylum by the Captain Regent before escaping by night toward the Adriatic.

events.title: Lincoln made an honorary citizen
events.summary: Abraham Lincoln accepted honorary citizenship of San Marino, writing that 'although your dominion is small, your State is nevertheless one of the most honored, in all history' - the beginning of US recognition of the republic.

events.title: First treaty of friendship with Italy
events.summary: After Italian unification, San Marino and the new Kingdom of Italy concluded a treaty of friendship in 1862 confirming the republic's independence, the first of the agreements that secure its sovereignty as an enclave.

figures.name: Giuseppe Garibaldi
figures.role: Risorgimento general sheltered by San Marino
figures.blurb: Hero of Italian unification who, fleeing the fall of the Roman Republic in 1849, was granted asylum in San Marino with his followers and his wife Anita before escaping pursuit. His refuge there became part of the republic's tradition of sanctuary.

figures.name: Abraham Lincoln
figures.blurb: President of the United States who in 1861 accepted honorary citizenship offered by San Marino's Captains Regent, praising the republic as 'one of the most honored, in all history' and a proof that republican government could endure.

pullquote.text: Although your dominion is small, your State is nevertheless one of the most honored, in all history.
pullquote.attribution: Abraham Lincoln to the Captains Regent of San Marino, 7 May 1861

eras.title: The Arengo, Fascism and the World Wars
eras.standfirst: A democratic revolution in 1906 ended centuries of oligarchy; two decades of fascism and a world war followed, in which neutral San Marino was bombed yet sheltered a hundred thousand refugees.
body: By the early twentieth century the Grand and General Council had hardened into an oligarchy: under the Statutes of 1600 it had come to co-opt its own members for life, concentrating power in a few wealthy landowning families. In 1906 a reform movement summoned the Arengo, the ancient assembly of household heads, for the first time in three centuries. On 25 March 1906 the assembled citizens voted overwhelmingly to abolish the lifelong co-option of councillors, and on 10 June 1906 San Marino held its first modern democratic elections, transforming the republic into an electoral democracy.

Democracy proved fragile. Amid the unemployment and inflation that followed the First World War - during which the republic stayed neutral and sent only volunteers - the Sammarinese Fascist Party seized control in 1923, dissolved the Grand and General Council, and ruled in step with Mussolini's Italy for two decades. Fascist government in San Marino did not long outlast its Italian patron: three days after Mussolini's fall, on 28 July 1943, popular pressure brought down the party's rule, a day the republic still commemorates as the fall of fascism.

In the Second World War San Marino held to a hard-won neutrality, and turned itself into a vast refuge. Though its native population numbered fewer than fifteen thousand, the republic gave asylum and shelter to more than a hundred thousand displaced people from the surrounding war zone, among them Jews who escaped deportation. Neutrality did not spare it: on 26 June 1944 a British air raid, the result of faulty intelligence later acknowledged, killed some sixty people. In September 1944 fighting between German and Allied forces passed across the territory before the republic was liberated and its neutrality restored.

events.title: The Arengo and the end of oligarchy
events.summary: Summoned for the first time in three centuries, the Arengo of household heads voted on 25 March 1906 to abolish lifelong co-option of councillors, opening the way to democracy.

events.title: First modern democratic elections
events.summary: Following the Arengo vote, San Marino held its first modern democratic elections on 10 June 1906, ending centuries of oligarchic rule by co-opted councillors.

events.title: Fascist seizure of power
events.summary: Amid postwar economic distress the Sammarinese Fascist Party took control in 1923 and dissolved the Grand and General Council, ruling the republic for two decades in step with Mussolini's Italy.

events.title: The bombing of San Marino
events.summary: Despite the republic's neutrality, a British air raid on 26 June 1944, based on faulty intelligence later admitted, killed about sixty people in the small state.

events.title: Refuge for 100,000 and liberation
events.summary: Neutral San Marino, with a population under 15,000, sheltered more than 100,000 wartime refugees, including Jews; after fighting crossed its soil in September 1944 it was liberated and its neutrality restored.

figures.name: Domenico Maria Belzoppi
figures.blurb: Serving as Captain Regent in 1849, he granted Giuseppe Garibaldi and his followers asylum in San Marino on condition that they renounce their cause within its borders, upholding the republic's tradition of sanctuary at the risk of foreign occupation.

figures.name: The wartime refugees of San Marino
figures.blurb: Displaced civilians from the surrounding war zone, including Jews who escaped deportation, who found asylum on Mount Titano during the Second World War, far outnumbering the republic's own people and embodying its long tradition of refuge.

pullquote.text: Over 100,000 people received asylum, including a group of Jews saved from the concentration camps.
pullquote.attribution: On San Marino's wartime hospitality, native population then under 15,000

eras.title: The Modern Republic: the UN, Europe and the Euro
eras.standfirst: The world's oldest republic took its place among the family of nations, joining the United Nations and adopting the euro while keeping the independence its treaties with Italy guarantee.
body: After the Second World War San Marino settled into the life of a modern parliamentary democracy, still governed by the Grand and General Council and its two Captains Regent, and still entirely surrounded by the Italian Republic. Its independence continued to rest on the treaties of friendship and good-neighbourliness with Italy that began in 1862 and were renewed in 1939 and in later decades, agreements that guarantee the enclave's sovereignty while regulating customs, security and the practical realities of a state embedded within another.

The post-war era brought San Marino into the wider world. It joined the Council of Europe, and on 2 March 1992 - following the UN Security Council's recommendation in Resolution 744 of 25 February 1992 - it was admitted to the United Nations, taking its seat as a full and equal member state, a striking arrival for a republic of a few tens of thousands of people on a mountaintop.

European integration reached even this enclave. Long tied to the Italian lira, San Marino adopted the euro and, under a monetary agreement with the European Union, secured the right to use the single currency as legal tender and to mint a limited number of euro coins bearing its own national designs - prized by collectors the world over. The current agreement, in force since 2012, replaced an earlier one of 2000. More than seventeen centuries after a stonemason is said to have climbed Mount Titano, San Marino endures: a sovereign republic, a UN member, and by its own proud and widely accepted claim the oldest such state on earth.

events.title: Admission to the United Nations
events.summary: On the recommendation of Security Council Resolution 744 (25 February 1992), San Marino was admitted to the United Nations on 2 March 1992 as a full member state.

events.title: Treaties of friendship with Italy
events.summary: San Marino's sovereignty as an enclave is secured by treaties of friendship and good-neighbourliness with Italy first concluded in 1862 and renewed in 1939 and later, regulating relations while guaranteeing independence.

events.title: Member of the Council of Europe
events.summary: San Marino joined the Council of Europe, one of the steps by which the ancient republic took its place among the institutions of postwar Europe while keeping its full sovereignty.

events.title: Euro monetary agreement
events.summary: Under a monetary agreement with the European Union, in force since 2012 in place of an earlier 2000 accord, San Marino uses the euro as official currency and mints its own euro coins with national designs.

figures.name: The Grand and General Council
figures.blurb: San Marino's elected legislature of sixty members, which appoints the Captains Regent and the chief officers of state. Reformed from an oligarchic body into a democratic parliament after the Arengo of 1906, it remains the central institution of the modern republic.

figures.name: San Marino's euro coinage
figures.blurb: Under its monetary agreement with the European Union, the republic uses the euro and mints a limited number of coins bearing its own national designs. Sought after by collectors, the coins are a modern emblem of a tiny state's enduring sovereignty.

pullquote.text: San Marino uses the euro as its official currency under a Monetary Agreement with the European Union, and has the right to mint a set number of euro coins featuring its own national side.
pullquote.attribution: Central Bank of the Republic of San Marino

figures.name: Saint Marinus
figures.role: Founder and patron saint of San Marino
figures.blurb: The Christian stonemason who, by tradition, fled Diocletian's persecution and founded a free community on Mount Titano in 301, giving the republic its name and its founding ideal of liberty 'from both men'.

figures.name: Pope Clement XII
figures.role: Pope who restored San Marino's independence
figures.blurb: Pope who, after Cardinal Alberoni's occupation of 1739, condemned the seizure and on 5 February 1740 restored San Marino's independence, an act commemorated as the republic's liberation.

figures.name: Giuseppe Garibaldi
figures.blurb: Hero of Italian unification granted asylum in San Marino in 1849 as he fled the collapse of the Roman Republic, a celebrated episode in the little state's tradition of offering refuge.

figures.name: Abraham Lincoln
figures.blurb: US president who in 1861 accepted honorary citizenship of San Marino, hailing it as 'one of the most honored, in all history' and beginning the long friendship between the two republics.
